सं या सँ (संभूत) अथाषत् प्रथमतः कोनहु वंशक सभसँ प्राचीन गाम जे मूल 
कहौलक। वाक्त्य मे जाहह शब्दक आगाँ सँ या सं भेटत ओ शब्द मूल थीक। 
मूलक दूखण्ड अछि-मूल ओ मूलग्राम। यथा-सोदरपुर सररसव, दररहरा 
राजनपुरा, माण्डर या मडरै ससिंहौत्तल, खण्डवला या खडौरे एकमा पण्डुए 
महहन्द्रपुर, नरौने या नरउनय शक्क्त्तरायपुर। एहह सब मे क्रमशः सोदरपुर, 
दररहरा, माण्डर या मडरै, खण्डवला या खडौरे, पण्डुए नरौने मूल भेल। 
आओर सररसव, राजनपुरा, ससिंहौली, एकमा, महहन्द्रपुर, शक्क्त्तरायपुर

मूलक हेतु। एहहना सभ मूलक संग बुझवाक छथक। पंजीकार लोकहन अपन

दूषण पञ्जी THE BLACK BOOK || 253 
 
 
सकराढ़ी सँ अथाषत् लोहना मूलग्राम भेल ओ सकराढ़ी (सकहडवाड) मूल 
छथक। एहह मूलक जुडाओन झाक बालक दयानाथ झाक पुत्री मे लाल प्र0 
डोमनक हववाह अस्तु हुनक चारू बालक दयानाथ झाक दौहहत्र (नाती) 
भेलाह तँय दौ। दौहहत्रक संणक्षप्त रूप त्तलखल अछि। ताहह सँ आँगा त्तलखल 
अछि सररसव सोदरपुर सँ श्यामनाथ ‘दु दौ’। अथाषत् श्यामनाथक पुत्री मे 
दयानाथक हववाह अस्तु बुझबाक छथक जे श्यामनाथक दौहहत्री मे लाल 
छमश्रक हववाह तहनक पुत्र चारू भाय अथाषत् दौहहत्री पुत्र तँय श्यामनाथक 
आगाँ ‘दु दौ’ त्तलखल भेटत। आगाँ बढ़ी-यज्ञवती लाल छमश्रक धमषपत्नी 
िलन्खन्ह तँय त्तलखल जे यज्ञवती पुत्र अथाषत् गुणानन्दादद चारर भाय। 
सुताच अथाषत् यज्ञवतीक पुत्री परंच हुनका पुत्री नपहिं िलन्खन्ह तँय सुताक 
आगाँ हकिु नपहिं त्तलखल अछि। 
द्रिव्य-अपवाद: कतहु-कतहु सुताक आगाँ नाम नपहिं अथाषत् सुताक रूप जे 
नाम अछि तहनका बालक नपहिं। अथाषत् जहनका एकहुटा पुत्र नपहिं ततहु सुता 
त्तलखल जाईत अछि। 
आब आगाँ बढ़ी: श्रीमान् गुणानन्द सुतौ नागेश्वर लक्ष्मीश्वरौ मल्हनी देशे 
परशरमा वासी सररसव सोदरपुर सँ डोमन सुत दामोदर दौ सतौली सतलखा 
सँ रिुनाथ दु दौ। सुताच गंगा कौत्तशल्या यमहनकाकें। एहह ठाम गुणानन्द 
छमश्रक धमषपत्नीक नाम नपहिं त्तलखल अछि। परंच सुताच अथाषत् हुनक 
तीनटा पुत्रीक नाम अछि। आओर हुनका लोकहनक हववाहक उल्लेख अछि 
जे आद्या नाहर वासी नरसाम बत्तलयास सँ अनन्त सुत परमानन्द पत्नी। ए 
सुतौ मनबोध भगवानदेवौ। एहहठाम ‘आद्या’क अथष थीक प्रथम पुत्री गंगा 
जहनक हववाह नाहर गामक बत्तलयास मूलक नरसाम मूलग्रामक अनन्तक 
पुत्र परमानन्द सँ जाहह परमानन्दक दु बालक मनबोध ओ भगवानदेव एहह 
तरहें बाँकी पुत्री कें हववाहक उल्लेख अछि।

नामक पूवष सेहो अपरा त्तलखल भेटत-तात्पयष हुनक दोसर वा तेसर हववाह। 
धरमपुर, श्रीपुर, नत्तशरा (हवेली), आसंगा (आसजा), सुलतानपुर, सूयाषपुर, 
मल्हनी, हनशंखुड (हनशंकपुर), हराउत, िई, कवखण्ड, काकयोहन इत्यादद 
परगन्नाक उल्लेख भौगोत्तलक दृछि सँ कएल गेल अछि जे कोन गाम, कोन 
क्षेत्र मे अवस्स्थत अछि। ई परगन्ना सभ देशक आजादीक पूवष देशक नाम सँ 
सेहो बुझल जाइत िल। तँय धरमपुर देशे सुलतानपुर देशे पुस्तक मे त्तलखल 
भेटत। 
पंजी प्रबंधक पृष्ठभूछम 
आयाषविषक (आदर देवाक योग्य) सवाेत्कृि स्वरूप छमछथला अपन हनस्तुकी 
वा ठोस ;ब्वदहपतउ वत ॅैवसपकद्ध हवचारक हेतु दुहनयाँ भरर मे सुख्यात 
रहल अछि। एहह ठामक जीवन दशषन धमषप्राण लोकक हेतु मागषदशषक त्तसद्ध 
होएत आएल अछि। गूढ़ धमषशास्त्रीय वचनक व्याख्याक हेतु छमछथलाक 
व्यवहार प्रमाण बनल-धमषस्य हनणषयो ज्ञेयो छमछथलाव्यवहारतः। समस्त 
शास्त्रीय परम्पराक पालन समयानुसार संस्काररत कए संशोछधत ओ 
संवर्द्द्धिंत कए छमछथला भारतीय सनातन धमी समाज कें युगोछचत मागषदशषन 
करवा मे समथष रहल अछि। मानवजीवनक हवकास यात्रा मे पुंसवन संस्कार 
सँ लऽ कें मृत्योपरान्त सम्पाददत संस्कार सभ पर सनातन धमषग्रन्थ ओ 
स्मृछतग्रन्थाददसम मे जे हनदेश प्राप्त अछि ताहह मे छमछथलाक हवद्वानक 
अदद्वतीय योगदान अछि। सवाेपरर हववाहक प्रसंग मे हकएक तँ हववाह 
आओर पररवार, मानव जाछत मे आत्म संरक्षण, वंशवृत्तद्ध आओर जातीय 
जीवनक हनरंतरता कें सुरणक्षत रखबाक सवषप्रमुख साधन थीक। अहनत्य 
मनुष्य एहह माध्यमें अमरता प्राप्त करैि। ब्रह्मपुराण मे कहल गेल अछि-

‘‘अमृते नामरा देवाः पुत्रेण ब्राह्मणादयः ब्रह्म0पु0 104/9, ऋग्वेद मे पुत्र 
द्वारा अमृतत्व प्रात्प्तक उल्लेख अछि-प्रजात्तभरग्ने अमृतत्वश्याम-छम0तै0 
सं0-1-4-46-1 इत्यादद। हववाहक माध्यमें मनुष्य सन्तान प्राप्त कऽ कें 
अपनाकें हवस्ताररत करैत अछि ओ संगपहिं अमरता सेहो। एहह हेतुएँ संस्कृत 
मे बच्चाकें संतछत सन्तान तनय इत्यादद कहल जाईत अछि। ई सभटा शब्द 
हवस्तारवाची तनु धातु सँ बनल अछि। सन्तानक रूप मे हपताक पुनजषन्म 
होईत िैक। एक ददश जँ मनुष्य कें अवश्यम्भावी मृत्युक दुख िैक तँ दोसर 
ददश इहो सन्तोष रहैत िैक जे सन्तान द्वारा ओ अनन्त काल तक जीहवत 
रहत। एहह हेतुएँ हववाह कें समाजक केन्द्रीय सिा मानल गेल िैक। अस्तु, 
एतेक जे महत्वपूणष कायष हववाह, जाहह पर श्रृछि हटकल अछि से कोन रूपें 
कएल जाए, कोन कन्या सँ हववाह कए सन्तान प्राप्त करी, ताहह पर 
याज्ञवल्क्त्यादद ऋहषगण जे अपन छममांशा हवत्तभन्न स्मृछत ग्रन्थ सभ मे 
प्रस्तुत कएने िछथ तकर सवषतोभावेन पालन छमछथलहह टा मे संभव भए 
सकल अछि। 
हववाहक प्रसंग ऋहष लोकहनक द्वारा देल गेल हनषेध-हवधेय वचन अछि से 
हनम्नोक्त्त अछि-(1) वर-कन्या स्वजाछत हो (2) समान गोत्र ओ प्रवरक 
नहह हो (3) कन्या जाहह-जाहह पूवषज सँ िठम स्थान धरर हो से वरक हपतृ 
पक्षक सहपण्ड नपहिं हो ततः पर कन्या जाहह-जाहह पूवषज सँ पाँचम स्थान 
धरर हो से वरक मातृ पक्षक सहपण्ड नपहिं हो अथाषत् असहपण्डा कन्या सँ 
हववाह करी सहपण्डताक पररछध हनधाषररत अछि। ‘‘पंचमात् सप्तमात् उध्वां 
मातृतः हपतृतस्तथा सहपण्डता हनविेत सवषवणेष्वयं हवछध’’। एकरा दोसर 
रूपें बुझक हो तँ हनम्नोक्त्त व्याख्या देखू- मातृतः पंचमी त्यक्त्त्वा हपतृतः 
सप्तमी भजेत् (पूवषकाल मे त्यजेत्)। वरक दूटा कुल होइत अछि हपतृकुल 
ओ मातृकुल। हपता- हपतामहाददक हपतृकुल ओ मातृकुल वरक हेतु

दूषण पञ्जी THE BLACK BOOK || 257 
 
 
हपतृकुल भेल-एहह मे जँ कोनो हपतृपक्षक सहपण्ड पूवषज सँ कन्या िठम 
स्थान धररक अभ्यन्तर िछथ वा मातृपक्षक सहपण्ड पूवषज सँ कन्या पाँचम 
स्थान धररक अभ्यन्तर िछथ तँ ओहह कन्या सँ वरक वैवाहहक अछधकार 
नपहिं। मूलाथष अछि सहपण्डता हनवृछत- असहपण्डाकन्या हववाहाथष अनुमोददत 
कएल गेल िछथ। सहपण्डताक सीमा स्मृछत सँ हनधाषररत कएल गेल अछि। 
वरक हपतृ पक्ष मे सात पीढ़ी धरर एकर अछतररक्त्त आन कोनो दोसर 
प्रकारक सहपण्डताक चचाष स्मृछत ग्रन्थादद मे नपहिं अछि। (4) कन्या-वरक 
कठमामक (हवमाता अथाषत् सतमाय) सन्तान नहह हो। (5) वरक मातामह 
तथा हपतामहक सन्तान कन्या नपहिं हो (6) ज्येष्ठ कें अहववाहहत रहला पर 
हववाह नपहिं करबाक िोषणाक बादे िोटक हववाह हो (7) जीहवत पत्नीक 
सोदर बहहन सँ हववाह नपहिं हो। जीव हवज्ञानक गुणसूत्रक ;ब्ॅीतवउवेवउेद्ध 
त्तसद्धान्त आ धमषशास्त्रक सहपण्डता मे एहह सन्दभष मं एकवाक्त्यता अछि। 
अस्तु, हववाहक हेतु उपरोक्त्त हनषेध ओ हवधेय हवज्ञान सम्मत अछि। 
वतषमान जीवहवज्ञान एवं आनुवंत्तशकी त्तसद्ध करैि जे ‘‘जीन’’ अपन समस्त 
गुण-दोषक संगे कुल परंपरा मे अग्रसर होइत रहैि। सातम (संशोछधत 
िठम) ओ पाँचम पीढ़ीक बाद समस्त गुणसूत्रताक हनवारण होइत िैक। 
एहह सभ हनदेशक एक मात्र उद्येश्य अछि उिरोिर गुणविा सँ पररपूणष 
सन्तान प्राप्त हो जे संसारक श्रीवृत्तद्ध मे अपन सवषश्रेष्ठ क्षमता लगा सकछथ। 
वस्तुतः ई संसार ईश्वरक एक प्रयोगशाला थीक। जतऽ प्रत्येक छचन्तनशील 
समाज अपन सुछचन्न्तत सुहवचाररत त्तसद्धान्त कें व्यावहाररक रूप देबा लेल 
उत्कंदठत रहैत अछि। अस्तु एहह सब त्तसद्धान्तक व्यावहाररक परीक्षण 
तखनपहिं संभव जखन सहपण्डताक ज्ञान प्रात्प्तक हेतु वर ओ कन्याक 
यावतो पररचय उपलब्ध हो तँय वंश परम्पराक इछतहास राखब परमावश्यक 
रामायण, महाभारत, वायुपुराणादद ग्रन्थ मे हम देखैत िी जे हवत्तभन्न वंशक

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 10 — हलायुध वंशावली
records.text:
258 || गजेन्द्र ठाकुर GAJENDRA THAKUR 
 
 
वंशावली अत्यन्त प्रयासपूवषक त्तलखल गेल अछि। रामायण मे मयाषदा 
पुरुषोिम राम ओ जगज्जननी सीताक हववाहक प्रसंग मे हुनका लोकहनक 
कुलक वणषन क्रमशः वत्तशष्ठ ओ सांकाश्याक राजा कुशध्वज कएने िछथ। 
प्रदाने हह मुहनश्रेष्ठ कुलं हनरवशेषतः। 
वक्त्तव्यं कुलजातेन तन्न्नबोधमहामते।। बाल्मीकी रामायण- 1/7/12 
ताहह सँ आगाँ बढ़ी तँ इशाक सातम् शताब्दी मे हवद्यमान कुमाररल भट्टक 
रछचत तंत्रवार्तिंक मे हववाह संबंध मे प्रवृछत-हनवृछत तय करबाक आधार रूप 
मे समूह लेख्यक परम्पराक संकेत प्राप्त होईि- 
हवत्तशिे नैव हह प्रयत्नेन महाकुलीनाः परररक्षन्न्त आत्मानम। अनेनैव हह 
हेतुना राजात्तभब्राषह्मणैश्च स्वहपतृ हपतामहाददपारम्पयाषहवस्मरणाषथां समूह 
लेख्याहन प्रवर्तिंताहन। तथा च प्रछत कुलं गुणदोषस्मरणािदनुरूपाः प्रवृछत 
हनवृियो दृश्यन्ते।। तन्त्रवार्तिंक-1-11-21 
अथष: ओहह समय मे ब्राह्मण ओ क्षत्रीय लोकहन अपन-अपन हपता-
हपतामहाददक परम्पराक स्मरण रखवाक हेतु ओ हववाहक अवसर पर 
हवत्तभन्न कुलक गुण-दोषक हवचार कए नवीन संबंधक हेतु सम्मुख वा 
हवमुख होएबाक लेल प्रयासपूवषक वंश पररचय ‘‘समूह लेख्य’’क रूप मे 
रखैत िलाह। ई ‘‘समूह लेख्य’’ कुलक मान्यजन रखैत िलाह आ बेर 
पडला पर उपस्स्थत करैत िलाह। शताछधक वषष धरर एहहना चलैत रहल। 
परंच जेना हक पूवषपहिं त्तलखल अछि म्लेच्छ यवनक उत्पीडन सँ जखन समाज 
छिन्न-त्तभन्न होमय लागल। एकहह कुलक त्तभन्न-त्तभन्न शाखा देश-देशान्तर 
मे पलायन करैत गेलाह तखन ‘‘समूह लेख्यक’’ परम्परा सेहो हवनि भऽ 
गेल। बहुतो हववाह शास्त्र हवरूद्ध होईत रहल। कतहुँ त्राण नपहिं। समाज

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 10 — हलायुध वंशावली
records.text:
दूषण पञ्जी THE BLACK BOOK || 259 
 
 
क्त्लेश मे हनमग्न िल, एहह दुःकालक एकटा िटनाक चचाष करब अनुछचत 
नपहिं। तेरहम श्ताब्दीक उिराद्धष मे जखन छमछथला मे काणाषट वंशक शासन 
िल तऽ महाराज हररससिंहदेवक राज्य सभाक धमाषध्यक्षक पद पर 
धमषशास्त्रक प्रत्तसद्ध हवद्वान हररनाथ उपाध्याय पदासीन िलाह। दुयाेग सँ 
समुछचत पररचयक अभाव मे हुनक हववाह गंगोर मूलक नयनाथक कान्या सँ 
अनछधकार मे भऽ गेलैन्न्ह आ हववाहोपरान्त शीघ्रे बूढ़-पुराणक मूँह सँ जे 
हववाहक अवसर पर हवलम्ब सँ उपस्स्थत भेल िलाह तहनका सँ एहह 
दुिषटनाक ज्ञान भेलैन्न्ह। सन्तप्त हृदय सँ ओ पत्नी कें अपन वृद्ध माताकें 
आश्रय मे रान्ख राजधानी चत्तल अएलाह। पत्नीकें सेहो एहह दुिषटनाक ज्ञान 
भेलैन्न्ह। विषमान जीवनकें हनस्सार बुणझ त्तशवाराधना मे लाहग गेलीह। 
प्रछतददन गामक एकटा त्तशव मन्न्दर मे पूजाक हेतु जाछथ आ वृद्ध सासुक 
सेवा करछथ। एहह मे जीवन बीतऽ लगलैन्न्ह। दीिषकाल बीछत गेल। एक बेर 
वषाषक समय मे पूजाक हेतु मन्न्दर गेलीह। पूजोपरान्त जे गाम ददत्तश हवदा 
भेलीह तऽ वषाष प्रारंभ भए गेलैक ओ ठमहक गेलीह। तखनहह एक हरवाहा 
वषाष सँ बचवाक हेतु मन्न्दर मे आएल। वषाष रूहक गेला पर दूनू गोटे एकपहिं 
बेर मन्न्दर सँ प्रस्थान कएलैन्न्ह। ताहह स्स्थछतक लांिन लगाए गामक हकिु 
अवण्ड युवक द्वारा पस्ण्डतजी कें एहह छमथ्या कलंकक सूचना देल गेलैन्न्ह। 
ई सुहन पस्ण्डताईन अत्यन्त व्यछथत भए गेलीह ओ तकर हनवारणाथष राज 
दरबार गेलीह आ अपनाकें हनष्कलंक त्तसद्ध करबाक प्राथषना राज सभा सँ 
कएलैन्न्ह। ताहह ददनक जे प्रायन्श्चिक हवधान िल ताहह मे प्रथम प्रयासें ओ 
दोषी त्तसद्ध भेलीह। परंच ओ तऽ परम पहवत्र िलीह, मात्र स्वजना हववाहक 
चलैत जे दोष हुनका मे व्याप्त िलैन्न्ह ताहह दोषें ओ प्रायन्श्चत्ति भेल िलीह। 
पुनश्च ओ अपन समस्या ओहह समयक सवषश्रेष्ठ हवदुषी धमषशास्त्रक ममषज्ञा 
लन्खमा ठकुराइनक (ई लन्खमा श्रीपुर प्रगन्नाक फूलसरा ग्रामक दररहरा 
मताओन मूलक छममांशक रछत छमश्रक आत्मजा िलीह ओ अलयवार मूलक

records.text:
260 || गजेन्द्र ठाकुर GAJENDRA THAKUR 
 
 
महामहोपाध्याय रामेश्वरक धमषपत्नी िलीह) शरणागत भए अपना समस्या 
रखलैन्न्ह। ओ हहनक जीवनक पूवाषपर िटना जाहन प्रायन्श्चिक मंत्र ‘‘नाहं 
चाण्डाल स्वजना गामी’’ कें बदत्तल ‘‘पछतर्द्भिंन्ना नाहं चाण्डाल 
स्वजनागामी’’ कए देलन्खन्ह। एहह बेर ओ हनदाेषी त्तसद्ध भेलीह। 
उपरोक्त्त िटना सँ प्रभाहवत भए महाराज हररससिंहदेव अपन राज्यक हवद्वान 
ब्राह्मण लोकहन कें आमंत्तत्रत कए हुनका लोकहनकें एहह लेल प्रेररत 
कएलन्खन्ह जे पूवषक समूह लेख्यक परम्पराक हवनि भेला सँ जे धमषक लोप 
भऽ रहल अछि तकर समाधान ताकछथ। ब्राह्मण लोकहन दीिषकाल धरर एहह 
पर कायष कए एहह हनष्कषष पर पहुँचलाह जे समस्त ब्राह्मण, क्षत्रीय ओ कणष 
कायस्थ समाजक वंशी पररचयक संग्रहणक ओ संरक्षणक दाछयत्व 
समाजक योग्यतम व्यक्क्त्तकें देल जाए। ओ व्यक्क्त्त धमषशास्त्रज्ञ होछथ ओ 
समाजक धमषक रक्षाक हेतु तत्पर रहछथ। हववाहक अवसर पर हुनकपहिं सँ 
स्वस्स्त पाहब ब्राह्मणादद वगष हववाह कररथ। पररचयक पुस्तक पंजी कहौलक 
ओ पररचय रखहनहार व्यक्क्त्त पंजीकारक संज्ञा सँ हवभूहषत भेलाह। पहहल 
पंजीकार पण्डुआ ततैल (महहन्द्रपुर) मूलक सदुपाध्याय गुणाकर भेलाह 
जहनक पररचय एहह पुस्तकक लेखकक पूवषजक पररचयक सन्दभष मे आएल 
िैन्न्ह। कालान्तर मे हुनक अनेक, त्तशष्योपत्तशष्य भेलैन्न्ह। पूवष मे पंजीकारक 
सहयोगक हेतु गामे-गाम पररचेता सेहो होएत िलाह। जकर स्वरूप 
हैवहनधरर देखबा मे अबैत िल। महाराज हररससिंहदेवक सभा मे जे 
पंजीकारक दाछयत्व सदुपाध्याय गुणाकर कें भेटल िलैन्न्ह ताहह सन्दभषक 
श्लोक हनन्मोक्त्त अछि: 
नन्देन्दु शुन्यं शत्तश शाकवषे (1019)। 
तत्रावणस्यधवले मुहनछतथ्यधस्तात्।।

records.text:
दूषण पञ्जी THE BLACK BOOK || 261 
 
 
स्वाती शनैश्चरददने सुपुणजत लग्ने। 
श्री नान्यदेव नृपछतव्यषदधीत वास्तं।।1।। 
शास्तानान्यपछतवषभूव नृपछतः 
श्री गंगदेवोनृपतत्सूनून्नरससिंह देव 
हवजयी श्री शक्क्त्तससिंह तत्सुतः 
तत् सूनू खलु रामससिंह हवजयी 
भूपालवन्तः सुतो जातः श्री हररससिंहदेव नृपछतः 
काणाषट चूडामणणः।।2।। 
श्रीमन्तं गुणवन्तमुिमकुलस्नाया हवशुद्धाशयं 
संजातानु गवेषणोत्सुक तयात्सवाषनु व्यक्क्त्तक्षषमं। 
चातुयषश्चतुराननश्च प्रछतहनछध कृत्वाच्चतुद्धाषछममां पंचाददत्य कुलान्न्वता 
हववजया ददत्ये ददौ पंणजकाम्।।3।। 
भूपालावहन मौत्तल रत्न मुकुटालंकार हहरांकुर ज्योत्सो ज्वाल यटाल भाल 
शत्तशत्तभः त्तललंच चंचल मुखः। शोभा भाणज गुणाकरे गुणवतां मानन्द 
कन्दोदरे। दृिवासभां हररससिंहदेव नृपछतः पाणौददौ पंणजकाम्।।4।। 
दृिवासभां श्री हररससिंहदेव हवचायषछचिे गुणणने सहहष्णौ, गुणाकरे मैछथलवंशे 
जाते पंजी ददौ धमष हववेचणाथषम।।5।। 
वालाùढॅयुग्म शत्तश सत्म्मत शाकवषे

THEORETICAL FOUNDATIONS

Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 7 
 
Chapter 1 
Genome Mapping of Mithila: The Pañjī System — 
Genealogical Theories Study (Volume I) 
GENOME MAPPING OF MITHILA 
450 A.D. – 2009 A.D. 
The Mithila Pañjī System: A Complete Study from the Perspective 
of Genealogical Theories 
*Based on: Intro_1.pdf (Gajendra Thakur, Nagendra Kumar Jha&Vidyanand 
Jha)* 
PREFATORY NOTE & ABSTRACT 
The Mithila Pañjī system — literally 'Genome Mapping' of Mithila from 450 
A.D. to 2009 A.D. — is one of the most remarkable genealogical institutions 
in the history of human civilisation. Spanning over fifteen hundred years, it 
constitutes a continuously maintained, professionally administered, and 
socio-legally binding register of patrilineal and matrilineal descent lines of 
Maithili Brahmin families, later extended to Karna-Kayastha communities. 
This study examines the Pañjī system exhaustively through the lens of 
modern and classical genealogical theories: descent theory, kinship algebra, 
exogamy logic, genome mapping analogies, social memory theory, 
institutional genealogy, and the epistemology of genealogical knowledge. It 
also places the system comparatively within world genealogical traditions. 
Primary Text | Jīnoma Maipiṃga (450 A.D.–2009 A.D.) — Mitilāka Pañjī 
Prabandha 
Period Covered | 450 A.D. – 2009 A.D. (over 1,550 years of continuous 
record)

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 8 
Founder Institution | Mahārāja Harisimhadeva, Kāṇāṭa dynasty, Śaka 1248 / 
1326 C.E. 
First Pañjīkāra | Sudupādhyāya Guṇākara Jhā (of Paṇḍua Mūla) 
Register Type | Patrilineal descent (Gotra) + Matrilineal prohibitions 
(Sapiṇḍa) 
Covering Communities | Maithili Brahmin (Śrotriya, Yogya, Pañjībaddha) + 
Karna Kayastha 
Genealogical Scope | 32-ancestor Uṭheṛha; 16-Ṣaṣṭhī kinship computation 
Surviving Record Volume | 185 Pañjīs; 32 Śrotriya + 153 Yogya/Vaṃśaja 
I. THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK: GENEALOGY AS A 
FIELD 
1.1 Definitions and Disciplinary Background 
Genealogy, derived from the Greek genealogia (family history), is the study 
of the lines of descent from an ancestor. As an academic discipline it draws 
on anthropology, history, law, genetics, demography, and semiotics. Modern 
genealogical theory operates across three major paradigms: 
•Descent Theory (social anthropology): How societies construct and deploy 
lineal identity through patrilineal, matrilineal, or cognatic descent systems. 
•Kinship Algebra (structural anthropology): The formal, mathematical 
mapping of kinship relationships and marriage prohibition rules. 
•Social Memory & Archival Theory: How genealogical knowledge is 
produced, stored, contested, and transmitted across generations. 
The Mithila Pañjī is uniquely significant in all three paradigms. It is not 
merely a family history archive but a living legal-administrative instrument, a 
computational 
kinship 
device, 
and 
a 
social 
memory 
repository 
simultaneously. 
1.2 Classical Indian Genealogical Theory 
Indian genealogical thought has ancient roots in three traditions: 
•Vaṃśāvalī (genealogical narrative): Linear royal and Brahminic genealogies 
from the Purāṇas, listing descent from cosmogonic ancestors. 
•Gotra-Pravara system: The Vedic attribution of Ṛgvedic Ṛṣis as ancestral 
identity markers for exogamy management.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 9 
•Sapiṇḍa computation: The Dharmaśāstra-based calculation of shared bodily 
substance across generations for marriage prohibition. 
The Pañjī system synthesises all three. It begins with Gotra (Vedic Ṛṣi-
lineage), adds Mūla-village identity (akin to Vaṃśāvalī), and encodes 
Sapiṇḍa rules in the operative 32-ancestor Uṭheṛha document. It is thus a 
total genealogical system integrating narrative, juridical, and computational 
dimensions. 
II. DESCENT SYSTEM ANALYSIS 
2.1 Patrilineal Descent Architecture 
The Pañjī system is fundamentally patrilineal. Gotra — the primary identity 
unit — is defined explicitly: 'All members of a Gotra are descendants of a 
single ancient known Mahāpuruṣa.' The text states that in Maithili Brahmin 
society, Gotra is 'vaṃśa-bodhaka' (lineage-indicating), with each Gotra 
named after its earliest known patrilineal ancestor. 
The twenty Gotras enumerated are: Śāṇḍilya, Vatsa, Sāvarṇa, Kāśyapa, 
Rāśada, Bhāradvāja, Kātyāyana, Garga, Kauśika, Alāmbukākṣa, Kṛṣṇātreya, 
Gautama, 
Maudgalya, 
Vasiṣṭha, 
Kauṇḍinya, 
Upamanya, 
Kapila, 
Viṣṇuvṛddhi, Daṇḍī, and Jātūkarṇa. 
Of these, seven Gotras are numerically predominant and socially organised: 
Śāṇḍilya, Vatsa, Kāśyapa, Sāvarṇa, Rāśada, Bhāradvāja, Kātyāyana. The 
remaining thirteen are documented but smaller in population. 
2.2 Mūla: The Sub-Lineage Unit 
Below the Gotra level, the system deploys 167 Mūlas (primary villages/sub-
lineage units). The Mūla is the operationally crucial unit for marriage 
prohibition calculations. Within a single Gotra, there may be dozens of 
Mūlas — for instance, Śāṇḍilya Gotra alone contains Mūlas including 
Didhauṣa, Sarisaba, Mahuvā, Baulī, Khaṇḍabalā, Gaṅgolī, Jajalvāl, and many 
others. 
The document specifies 35 primary Mūlas (34 + the Khanāma branch of 
Fananda Mūla), organised in three tiers: Ayānta Śreṣṭha (13 elite), Dvitīya 
Śreṇī (13 second-rank), and Madhyama Mūla (8 middle). This hierarchical 
stratification is genealogically significant: it maps social rank onto descent 
categories.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 10 
2.3 Bījī Puruṣa: The Founding Ancestor 
Each Mūla traces descent to a Bījī Puruṣa (seed-ancestor), the earliest known 
male ancestor in that line. The text offers a detailed example: for Kāśyapa 
Gotra, 74 branches survive in Mithila, all traceable to an ancestor resident in 
Māṇḍara village. The genealogical reconstruction shows that even branches 
settled in different villages (Gaṛha, Maṅgauraunī) are shown by Pañjī 
evidence to be branches of the same Kula with a common Bījī Puruṣa. 
This concept of Bījī Puruṣa is the precise Indian analogue of what Western 
genealogical theory calls the 'progenitor' or 'founder ancestor' — the 
individual at the root of a documented descent tree. The Pañjī system is 
extraordinary in maintaining documented chains from Bījī Puruṣas dated as 
early as 400 C.E. (e.g., the mention of Mahāmahopādhyāya Tinayana Bhaṭṭa 
of Māṇḍara Mūla, circa 400 A.D.). 
2.4 Cognatic Descent in Matrilineal Sapiṇḍa Computation 
While the Pañjī is structurally patrilineal, it incorporates cognatic elements 
through the matrilineal Sapiṇḍa prohibition. The Pañcamī-Saptamī rule (from 
Manusmṛti) requires that the bride not be within five generations on the 
mother's side and seven generations on the father's side of the groom. This 
makes the system a bilateral or 'double descent' system for the purpose of 
marriage prohibition, even if only the patrilineal line is used for identity. 
This is theoretically important: the system operates as a patrilineal identity 
system plus a bilateral prohibition system. The 32-ancestor Uṭheṛha 
document, which maps 16 kinship nodes (Ṣaṣṭhī) from both patrilineal and 
matrilineal ancestors, is the technical instrument that operationalises this 
bilateral computation. 
III. KINSHIP ALGEBRA AND EXOGAMY 
COMPUTATION 
3.1 The 32-Ancestor Uṭheṛha: A Kinship Matrix 
The Uṭheṛha is the genealogical working document prepared for each 
prospective marriage. It maps 32 ancestors (hence 'battīsa mūlaka uṭheṛha') 
across seven patrilineal and five matrilineal generations. In contemporary 
genealogical and genetic terms, this is equivalent to a 5-generation pedigree 
chart covering all 32 great-great-great-grandparents.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 11 
The 16 Ṣaṣṭhī (kinship prohibition nodes) are computed from this chart. The 
text provides a complete enumeration: the 1st Ṣaṣṭhī is the bride's Vṛddha 
Prapitāmaha (great-great-great-grandfather); subsequent ones trace through 
all 16 specific kinship paths (paternally and maternally across patrilineal and 
matrilineal branches). 
3.2 Formal Rules of Exogamy 
The system encodes four formal rules for marital eligibility: 
•Rule 1 (Sapiṇḍa): The bride and groom must not share any of 16 Ṣaṣṭhī 
ancestors through the groom's patrilineal side. If they share one through the 
matrilineal side, marriage is permitted. 
•Rule 2 (Gotra): The bride and groom must not belong to the same Gotra. 
•Rule 3 (Pravara): The bride and groom must not share the same Pravara 
(Ṛgvedic Ṛṣi-cluster). 
•Rule 4 (Vipiāmātā): The bride must not be the daughter of the groom's 
maternal uncle. 
These rules constitute a formal prohibition algebra. The marriage space is 
defined as the set of all women who satisfy all four conditions 
simultaneously. The Pañjī system automates this computation through the 
Uṭheṛha document and the Pañjīkāra's verification. 
3.3 Pravara System: A Genetic-Lineage Marker 
The Pravara system classifies Gotras by their Ṛgvedic Ṛṣi-cluster. The text 
divides Maithili Brahmin Gotras into Tripravara (three-Ṛṣi ancestry) and 
Pañcapravara (five-Ṛṣi ancestry). The key rule: even if two Gotras share 
identical Pravara ancestors (as Vatsa and Sāvarṇa do — both deriving from 
Aurva, Cyavana, Bhārgava, Jamadagni, and Āplava), they cannot intermarry 
despite being distinct Gotras. 
From the standpoint of genealogical theory, this is a case of overlapping 
descent-group membership: two named Gotras with partially shared ancestral 
attribution. The prohibition is maintained because the shared Pravara implies 
closer-than-permitted common ancestry at the Ṛṣi level, even if the social 
Gotra designation differs.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 12 
3.4 Parallel with Population Genetics 
The document's title 'Genome Mapping' (Jīnoma Maipiṃga) is deliberately 
chosen. The authors argue that the Ṛṣi-genealogical system encoded in the 
Pañjī is functionally equivalent to chromosome mapping. Just as genetic 
science 
maps 
chromosomes 
to 
identify 
relatedness 
and 
prohibit 
consanguineous unions, the Pañjī maps genealogical 'chromosomes' (Gotra + 
Mūla + Sapiṇḍa chains) to identify biologically and socially too-close 
matches. 
This analogy has substance: the Pañcamī-Saptamī rule maps closely to what 
population geneticists call avoidance of second-degree (R = 0.) and third-
degree (R = 0.) consanguinity. Five maternal generations back reaches the 
5th degree of relatedness; seven paternal generations back reaches the 7th 
degree. This is well within the range of modern recommended minimum 
genetic distance for human marriage. 
IV. INSTITUTIONAL GENEALOGY: THE PAÑJĪ AS 
ADMINISTRATION 
4.1 Founding Event (1326 C.E.) and Legal Charter 
The institutional origin is precisely documented. In Śaka 1248 (1326 C.E.), 
under Mahārāja Harisimhadeva of the Kāṇāṭa dynasty, the Maithili Brahmin 
learned community collectively resolved that genealogical records could no 
longer be left to individual family memory. The catalyst was a scandal: 
Thaṃharinā Upādhyāya, a celebrated author of Dharmaśāstra texts including 
Smṛtisāra, committed an unknowing incestuous marriage (Svajana-vivāha) 
by marrying his own brother's daughter — precisely because his genealogical 
knowledge had lapsed. 
The solution was institutional: a royal decree creating a class of state-
appointed genealogists (Pañjīkāras) with the authority to issue 'Siddhānta 
Patra' (eligibility certificates) without which no Maithili Brahmin marriage 
could proceed. This was a state intervention in genealogical record-keeping 
without precedent in the ancient world outside of royal succession records. 
4.2 The Guṇākara Jhā Commission 
Sudupādhyāya Guṇākara Jhā of Paṇḍua Mūla (Śāṇḍilya Gotra) was 
appointed as the first chief Pañjīkāra. He and his team of Parichetās (field

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 13 
collectors) traversed the entire Mithila region, interviewing the senior 
member of each family and recording their genealogical knowledge. The 
collected data was cross-referenced to identify Bījī Puruṣas and Mūla 
villages. The resulting corpus became the authoritative genealogical 
database. 
This founding act closely resembles what institutional archival theory 
describes as a 'record-creation event' — the moment when tacit social 
knowledge is converted into formal documentary record. The Pañjī system is 
thus an early example of what contemporary genealogical archivists call a 
'genealogical information management system' (GIMS). 
4.3 The Pañjīkāra as Professional Genealogist 
The Pañjīkāra is a certified professional genealogist appointed by royal 
authority. The text is explicit about both the role's dignity and its ethical 
obligations. It warns against corruption, excessive fee extraction, and 
genealogical fabrication. The system creates 185 Pañjī records maintained by 
distinct Pañjīkāra families, operating as hereditary custodians. 
The document provides a striking ethical norm: 'The Pañjīkāra is a protector 
of culture, not a fee-extracting businessman.' It criticises Pañjīkāras who 
demand payment before issuing eligibility certificates, and calls for 
transparent succession planning to train the next generation. 
4.4 Record Format: Palm-Leaf to Digital 
The Pañjī books were originally written in Mithilākṣara (Tirhuta script) on 
palm-leaf (tāla patra). The text provides detailed instructions for manuscript 
preservation: protect from water, oil, and tight binding; sun-dry annually 
when the sun is in Leo (mid-August to mid-September). Each book could 
contain 500–600 palm-leaves bound with wooden covers and tied with cord 
('brahmagranthī' binding style). 
By 2000 C.E., a systematic digitisation project was launched: transcription of 
all surviving Pañjī manuscripts into Devanagari, with digital imaging of the 
palm-leaf originals onto DVD. This represents the full archival lifecycle: oral 
knowledge → formal manuscript → digitised preservation, spanning 1, years 
of genealogical record management.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 14 
V. GENEALOGICAL STRATIFICATION AND SOCIAL 
RANK 
5.1 The Three-Tier Brahmin Classification 
The Pañjī system classifies Maithili Brahmins into three genealogically 
defined tiers: 
•Śrotriya: The highest-rank Vedic scholars. Defined by Matṛkṣa (maternal 
side) of five generations and Pitṛkṣa (paternal side) of seven generations of 
maintained Vedic study. Divided into 8 Chaugolā (sub-ranks) with 32 Pañjīs. 
•Yogya ('Eligible'): The intermediate class. Fifteen sub-ranks with 153 
Pañjīs. 
•Pañjībaddha ('Registered'): All who are registered in the Pañjī. This is the 
baseline of legitimate genealogical standing. 
The text notes that those not registered (Jayavāt or 'fallen out') could not 
claim Vedic-hereditary marriage rights. This makes Pañjī registration a de 
facto citizenship document in Mithila's Brahminic legal order. 
5.2 Downward Mobility Through Misalliance 
A crucial genealogical-sociological feature: rank is not permanently fixed but 
can decline through mésalliance (marriage below one's rank). The text notes 
that 'Pañjī and rank descend downward' — a family's rank can fall if 
marriage alliances with lower-ranked families accumulate over generations. 
The first and second Śrotriya sub-ranks have already become extinct due to 
this mechanism. 
This is a powerful illustration of what sociologists call 'status attrition 
through hypogamy.' The genealogical record becomes the instrument of 
rank-tracking: the Pañjī does not merely record who one's ancestors were, but 
determines what social position one currently occupies based on the 
cumulative marriage record. 
5.3 Śākhā Pañjī: The Branch Record Innovation 
Under Mahārāja Mādhava Siṃha of Darbhanga (circa 1760 C.E.), a new 
genealogical instrument was introduced: the Śākhā Pañjī (branch register), 
which recorded separate sub-ranks within the broader Yogya and 
Pañjībaddha categories. The text is critical of this innovation, arguing it 
introduced social stratification and hierarchical abuse that were not part of

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 15 
the original Pañjī's spirit — which was purely genealogical (vaṃśāvalī), not 
socially ranking. 
This historical critique parallels debates in genealogical theory about the 
distinction between 'pure' descent recording and social ranking systems. The 
Śākhā Pañjī represents the moment when a genealogical archive was 
converted into a social stratification instrument. 
VI. SOCIAL MEMORY THEORY AND THE PAÑJĪ 
6.1 Oral to Written Genealogical Memory 
Before the institutionalisation of the Pañjī, genealogical knowledge was 
maintained by oral tradition within families. The text references Kumārila 
Bhaṭṭa's 7th-century Tantravārttika (commentary on Mīmāṃsā Sūtras of 
Jaimini) as the earliest reference to 'Samūha Lekhya' — written collective 
genealogical records maintained by noble families. This gives a 
chronological anchor: written genealogical record-keeping pre-dates the 
Pañjī system by at least seven centuries. 
Maurice Halbwachs's concept of 'collective memory' is directly applicable 
here. The Pañjī is not individual family memory but a socially constructed 
and institutionally validated collective memory. It transforms private 
genealogical knowledge into public authoritative record. 
6.2 The Pañjī as Counter-Memory 
The document reveals a remarkable feature: the Pañjī records social 'dirt' — 
marriages with women of uncertain or mixed lineage, inter-caste unions, 
children of courtesans (Devadāsī), and unions with Muslims, leather-workers 
(Carmakāra), and women from distant non-Brahmin communities. These are 
recorded not suppressed. 
The text provides examples numbered 1 through 97 in a table, showing: 
marriages with Yavana (Muslim) women (Daulatijahāṃ, number 11–12), 
marriages with Rājput women, marriages with Kocha (Assamese) royal 
family (example 9: 'Kocha Rāja Patnī'), marriages with 'parm Brahmin' (non-
Maithili Brahmin) women, and even unions with Ḥaḍinī Rudrāmaṭī. 
These records — what the text calls 'dūṣaṇa pañjī' (defect registers) — 
function as what Foucault would call counter-memory: records that preserve 
what normative genealogical narrative would erase. The document argues

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 16 
that after six generations, such 'impurities' cease to affect descent status — a 
form of genealogical limitation period. 
6.3 Genealogy as Historical Archive 
The Śākhā Pañjī's capacity as historical archive is illustrated by the 
Āryabhaṭṭa genealogy preserved within it. The text quotes a Pañjī entry 
tracing: Tinavana Bhaṭṭa (fl. 400 C.E.) → Udayabhaṭṭa → Vijayabhaṭṭa → 
(chain of descendants) → Āryabhaṭṭa (the mathematician-astronomer) → 
Udayabhaṭṭa → ... → Mahāmahopādhyāya Narasiṃha. 
Similarly, the lineage of Caitanya Mahāprabhu is traced: 'Tarapati 
Upādhyāya of Karamahā Taraunī Mūla went to Bengal; his disciple was 
Caitanya Mahāprabhu.' And the Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya entry (founder of 
Navya-Nyāya philosophy) records: 'Father died five years before Gaṅgeśa's 
birth' — a social fact preserved only in the genealogical record. These are 
unique historical data points not available from any other source. 
VII. COMPARATIVE GENEALOGICAL THEORY 
7.1 The Pañjī Among World Genealogical Systems 
The text makes a bold claim: 'This type of book has not been found in any 
country, any community, or any class in the world.' While this may be 
contested at the margins, the Pañjī system does present a unique combination 
of features unmatched by any other known genealogical institution. A 
comparative analysis: 
Feature | Pañjī System │ Comparable World Systems 
Duration of Continuous Record | 1,550+ years │ Medieval European 
heraldic records: 600-800 yrs 
Geographic Coverage | All of Mithila region │ National archives cover 
entire countries but not a single ethnic group 
Bilateral Computation | Yes (both paternal and maternal sides) │ Most: 
patrilineal only 
Legal Binding Power | Marriage without Siddhānta Patra invalid │ Only 
royal genealogies have comparable legal force 
Professional Custodians | Hereditary Pañjīkāras; state-appointed │ European 
heralds; state genealogists (limited) 
Social Stratification Use | Yes (Śrotriya/Yogya/Pañjībaddha) │ European 
armorial bearings; Chinese Clan registers

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 17 
Script/Medium | Tirhuta Mithilākṣara on palm-leaf; later paper │ European: 
parchment/paper; Chinese: wood block/paper 
Digitisation | Completed 2000-2009 C.E. │ European archives largely 
digitised 
7.2 Chinese Genealogical Tradition (Zúpǔ) 
The Chinese Zúpǔ (clan genealogy) tradition is the closest global parallel. 
Chinese clan registers maintained multi-generational patrilineal descent 
records for elite families, including biographical notices, property records, 
and ritual obligations. However, the Chinese system typically lacked a 
formal bilateral exogamy computation mechanism as sophisticated as the 
Pañjī's Ṣaṣṭhī system, and was not state-administered in the same juridical 
sense. 
7.3 European Heraldic Genealogy 
European heraldic genealogy, maintained by Heralds' Colleges from the 12th 
century onward, shares the Pañjī's concern with descent authentication and 
rank determination. However, European genealogy was primarily patrilineal 
(with exceptions in some royal succession laws), covered a much shorter 
time depth, and was not linked to exogamy computation. The European 
'Prohibition of Consanguinity' in canon law (to the 4th degree, later relaxed) 
is functionally analogous to the Pañcamī-Saptamī rule, though computed 
differently. 
7.4 Jewish Genealogy and the Cohen Gene 
The Jewish genealogical tradition of Kohanic descent (Kohanim claiming 
descent from Aaron, brother of Moses) presents an interesting parallel to the 
Gotra-Pravara system. In both cases, patrilineal descent from a named 
ancestor 
(Āroṇ 
/ 
specific 
Ṛṣis) 
confers 
religious 
status 
and 
endogamy/exogamy implications. Remarkably, genetic studies of Kohanim 
have confirmed that a specific Y-chromosome haplotype (the 'Cohen Modal 
Haplotype') is shared at high frequency, suggesting genuine patrilineal 
descent from a common ancestor. Similar genetic validation of Gotra-based 
Y-chromosome clustering has been explored in Indian genetic studies.

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 18 
VIII. EPISTEMOLOGY OF GENEALOGICAL 
KNOWLEDGE 
8.1 The Problem of Knowledge Decay 
The founding narrative of the Pañjī system is itself a story about genealogical 
knowledge failure. Thaṃharinā Upādhyāya — a learned Dharmaśāstrin — 
committed an incestuous union because genealogical knowledge had been 
lost. The institutional response was precisely to address the epistemological 
vulnerability of oral and individual memory. 
The text explicitly describes a typical family's genealogical knowledge at the 
time of the first Pañjī survey: 'Normally, six generations were known; some 
knew more; some knew only two or three generations.' The Pañjī system 
addresses this patchy knowledge by cross-referencing family accounts and 
reconstructing chains from Bījī Puruṣas upward and downward. 
8.2 The Archive-First Rule and Fabrication Risk 
The text contains a warning that has direct relevance to contemporary 
genealogical practice: Pañjīkāras are admonished to base all genealogical 
determinations on the actual archive, not on personal knowledge or 
improvised reconstruction. The document records a historical fabrication 
case and insists: 'The Pañjīkāra should abandon his burden of ignorance and 
narrow tendencies; [he] should follow the canonical injunctions earnestly, 
not his own invented rules.' 
This epistemological caution — privileging archival record over oral claim 
— is the central methodological principle of modern professional genealogy 
as codified by the Genealogical Proof Standard (GPS) of the Board for 
Certification of Genealogists (BCG, USA). The requirement for exhaustive 
search, complete citations, analysis of all evidence, and reasoned conclusions 
precisely mirrors the Pañjī system's insistence on archival primacy. 
8.3 Indexing, Cross-Reference, and the Notation System 
The Pañjī's notation system is a sophisticated information retrieval 
mechanism. Each entry is keyed by page number and line number (e.g., '3/2' 
= page 3, line 2). Cross-references are made using the notation of source 
location: 'Pull name X from page Y, line Z and bring to current entry.' This is

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 19 
functionally equivalent to relational database linking — records in different 
volumes linked by key identifiers. 
The Bījī Puruṣa lists in the Devānanda (Choṭī Jhā) Pañjī provide a 
remarkable index of founding ancestors by Mūla-village: 'From Gaṅgolī, Bījī 
Gaṅgādhara 1/3; from Khaṇḍabalā, Bījī Saṃkarṣaṇa 1/4; from Ekamā 
Valiyāsa, Bījī Dharaṇīdhara 1/12...' extending to hundreds of entries. This is 
effectively a master index of all Mūla-line progenitors in the database. 
IX. THE PAÑJĪ AND MODERN GENETICS: GENOME 
MAPPING ANALOGY 
9.1 The Authors' Explicit Genome Mapping Framework 
The document is titled 'Jīnoma Maipiṃga' (Genome Mapping) deliberately. 
The concluding theoretical passage states: 'In the current age of science, the 
determination of Chromosomes [guṇa-sūtra] is of this very form, and our Ṛṣi 
ancestors had already placed this [system] in a scientific Śāstric framework.' 
This is a significant claim: that the Gotra-Pravara-Sapiṇḍa system constitutes 
an early form of genetic relatedness avoidance. The claim merits serious 
genealogical-genetic analysis: 
•Gotra endogamy prohibition = avoidance of Y-chromosome identity 
(paternal haplotype): men of the same Gotra share a common male-line 
ancestor, and prohibiting Gotra-endogamy prevents close Y-chromosome 
relatedness. 
•Pravara prohibition = extension of patrilineal prohibition to shared Ṛgvedic 
ancestor-clusters: a more conservative form of the same Y-lineage 
avoidance. 
•Sapiṇḍa computation = avoidance of shared autosomal DNA within 5-7 
generations: both sides (maternal and paternal) mapped to detect excess 
identity-by-descent (IBD) at the autosomal level. 
9.2 Genetic Distance and the Ṣaṣṭhī System 
The 16 Ṣaṣṭhī nodes cover kinship relationships that in modern genetic 
notation correspond to: 
•1st Ṣaṣṭhī (Vṛddha Prapitāmaha): 5th degree ancestor. Expected IBD = 1/64 
≈ 1.6%

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 20 
•16th Ṣaṣṭhī (Mātāmahī's Mātṛmātāmaha): Similarly removed by 5+ degrees 
on maternal side. 
The rule that any shared Ṣaṣṭhī through the paternal side prohibits marriage, 
while a shared Ṣaṣṭhī through the maternal side permits marriage, reflects the 
asymmetric treatment of patrilineal and matrilineal descent in the system. 
From a genetic standpoint, both sides contribute equally to autosomal IBD — 
but the Sapiṇḍa system's logic is social as much as biological. 
9.3 Population Endogamy and Heterozygosity 
The Mithila Brahmin community's strict exogamy rules within a 
geographically bounded region would, from a population genetics standpoint, 
create a structured metapopulation with defined local endogamy (within the 
community) and regulated exogamy (between descent groups). This is 
precisely the type of marriage system that population geneticists model as 
'subdivided populations' — maintaining diversity through regulated gene 
flow between Gotras while restricting it within Gotras. 
The 20 Gotras with 167 Mūlas, spread across the Mithila region (roughly the 
Darbhanga-Madhubani-Sitamarhi-Saharsa districts of Bihar plus Terai 
Nepal), constitute a human population structured in a way that sustained 
genetic diversity while preventing deleterious consanguineous combinations 
— precisely the outcome that modern evolutionary genetics predicts for 
optimal fitness. 
X. THE PAÑJĪ AS LIVING SYSTEM: MODERN 
CHALLENGES 
10.1 Script as Genealogical Barrier 
The document identifies the biggest existential threat to the Pañjī system: the 
progressive loss of Mithilākṣara (Tirhuta) script literacy. All Pañjī 
manuscripts are written in Tirhuta; Pañjīkāras trained in Tirhuta can read 
them. But as fewer young people learn Tirhuta, the interpretive community is 
shrinking. Clients who appear before Pañjīkāras to verify marriage eligibility 
cannot read or interpret the records they are presented with. 
This is a classic case of what archival theorists call 'interpretive community 
breakdown' — the social network capable of reading and contextualising an 
archival corpus is dissolving. The authors' urgent advocacy for Devanagari

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 21 
transliteration (already undertaken for the major Pañjī manuscripts by 2009) 
is the correct archival response. 
10.2 Child Marriage, Polygamy, and Genealogical Anomaly 
The text candidly notes that the scientifically sound Pañjī system is 
compromised when child marriage and polygamy 'sit within it.' When a man 
simultaneously relates to another as both father-in-law and step-father 
through multiple marriages across families, the Ṣaṣṭhī computation becomes 
paradoxical. The text says the Pañjīkāra becomes 'helpless' — because such 
arrangements are technically Śāstra-approved yet genealogically anomalous. 
This identifies the limits of rule-based genealogical computation when social 
practices deviate from the system's assumptions. Modern computational 
genealogy faces analogous challenges with blended families, assisted 
reproduction, and legal versus biological parentage. 
10.3 Kshatriya Genealogical Loss 
The text notes that while the Pañjī was originally also maintained for 
Kshatriya (Gandharvatiyā Rājputs) — and the Varṇaratnākara of Jyotirísvara 
Thakura records 64 to 72 Rājpūt lineages — the Kshatriya Pañjī has now 
become extinct. They compensate by maintaining village-level exogamy 
rules ('not marrying into the grandfather's maternal village') without formal 
genealogical documentation. 
This illustrates the fragility of genealogical institutions: once the 
administrative 
machinery 
(Pañjīkāra 
appointment, 
royal 
patronage) 
disappears, the formal system collapses into informal rule-of-thumb 
practices. The Maithili Brahmin Pañjī has survived because of continuous 
professional custodianship; the Kshatriya system did not. 
10.4 Digital Future: From Palm-Leaf to Database 
The 2000-2009 digitisation project described in the document represents the 
system's entry into the digital genealogical age. The comprehensive digital 
imaging of palm-leaf manuscripts, combined with Devanagari transliteration, 
positions the Pañjī corpus for conversion into searchable genealogical 
databases. The DVD archive mentioned as an appendix to the book is the 
first step toward what could become a comprehensive relational genealogical 
database covering 1, years of Maithili Brahmin descent records.

The correspondence address provided (ggajendra@gmail.com) and the 
appeal to Pañjīkāras across Saurāṭha, Kakurauṛa, Naṃnaura, Baṃnaula 
(Nepal), and other centres to coordinate through the internet signals the 
authors' vision of a networked digital genealogical infrastructure maintaining 
the system's social function while expanding its accessibility. 
XI. UNIQUE CONTRIBUTIONS OF THE PAÑJĪ TO 
GENEALOGICAL THEORY 
11.1 Systemic Innovations 
The Pañjī system makes several innovations that are genuinely novel in the 
global history of genealogy: 
•1. Bilateral computation with asymmetric rules: The Uṭheṛha computes both 
patrilineal and matrilineal ancestry, but applies different prohibition depths 
(seven paternal vs. five maternal). No other pre-modern genealogical system 
has this precise bilateral asymmetric structure. 
•2. State-regulated professional genealogists: The appointment of Pañjīkāras 
by royal decree, with authority to issue legally binding marriage eligibility 
certificates, is unparalleled in pre-modern genealogical administration. 
•3. Mandatory registration as social citizenship: Pañjī registration 
(pañjībaddha) as the precondition for legitimate social standing within the 
community prefigures modern civil registration systems. 
•4. Integrated social-historical archive: The Pañjī simultaneously records 
genealogical data, social rank, educational qualifications (titles like 
Mahāmahopādhyāya, Kavīndra, etc.), geographical migration, and social 
anomalies — making it a comprehensive social history database. 
•5. Bibliographic genealogy: The explicit recording of scholarly titles, works 
authored, and royal appointments within genealogical entries (e.g., 'Gaṅgeśa 
Upādhyāya: Author of Tattvacintāmaṇi') creates a unique fusion of bio-
bibliography and genealogy. 
11.2 Theoretical Implications 
The Pañjī system challenges several assumptions commonly made in 
comparative genealogical theory: 
•That sophisticated genealogical institutions require literacy: The Pañjī 
emerged from oral traditions and was institutionalised before widespread

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 23 
literacy. Its survival required script expertise, but its social logic predates the 
script. 
•That state genealogical administration is a modern phenomenon: The Pañjī's 
royal founding in 1326 C.E. shows that state-administered mandatory 
genealogical registration is at least seven centuries old in South Asia. 
•That genetically informed exogamy rules require genetic science: The 
Pañjī's computations anticipate modern population genetics conclusions 
about optimal consanguinity avoidance, achieved through a social-religious 
framework. 
XII. CONCLUSION 
The Mithila Pañjī system, spanning 450 A.D. to 2009 A.D. and continuously 
maintained across this entire period, stands as one of the most sophisticated 
genealogical institutions in human history. Examined through the multi-lens 
framework of descent theory, kinship algebra, social memory theory, 
archival science, comparative genealogy, and population genetics, it reveals a 
level of genealogical engineering that was simultaneously scientifically 
sound, socially functional, historically significant, and institutionally 
resilient. 
Its core theoretical achievement is the integration of three genealogical 
functions that most systems separate: identity assignment (Gotra/Mūla/Bījī 
Puruṣa), exogamy computation (Sapiṇḍa/Ṣaṣṭhī/Pravara rules), and social 
rank tracking (Śrotriya/Yogya stratification). This integration, maintained 
through a hereditary professional class of Pañjīkāras operating under state 
authority, created a genealogical institution that outlasted the state that 
founded it, survived colonial disruption, and is now transitioning to digital 
preservation. 
The authors' framing of the Pañjī as 'Genome Mapping' is not merely 
metaphorical. The system encodes, through social-religious categories, the 
same information that modern population genetics seeks through molecular 
analysis: who is too closely related to whom for healthy reproduction, and 
how that relatedness is traced through time. The Pañjī system achieved this 1, 
years before the discovery of DNA. 
For genealogical theorists, the Pañjī system demands recognition as a distinct 
and advanced genealogical tradition — neither reducible to European 
heraldic genealogy, Chinese Zúpǔ traditions, nor modern civil registration,

Gajendra Thakur — 24 
but standing alongside them as a third archetype of institutionalised 
genealogical knowledge management, with its own unique logic, 
methodology, and civilisational achievement.

Chapter 2 
Mithila's Pañjī-Prabandha: Genealogical Theories Study 
(Volume II) 
MITHILA'S PAÑJI-PRABANDHA 
A Complete Study from the Perspective of Genealogical Theories 
Based on: Genome Mapping — Pañji-Prabandha of Mithila (450 AD–2009 
AD), Part 2 
Authors: Gajendra Thakur · Nagendra Kumar Jha · Pañjīkāra Vidyānanda 
Jha 'Mohanji' 
Abstract 
The Pañji-Prabandha of Mithila (mithilāka pañjī-prabandha) is the world's 
most sustained, institutionally organised, and continuously practised 
genealogical record-keeping system, with documented continuity from at 
least 450 AD to the present. This study examines the Pañji-Prabandha 
through the integrated lens of contemporary genealogical theory, genetic 
genealogy, socio-legal genealogy, documentary genealogy, and the classical 
Indian framework of vaṃśa-paramparā. Drawing on the Introduction 
(Bhūmikā) to Part 2 of Genome Mapping (2009/2012 edition), we analyse 
the system's theoretical foundations, structural architecture, methodological 
rigour, epistemological significance, and comparative standing among world 
genealogical traditions. The study demonstrates that the Pañji is not merely a 
caste registry but a comprehensive genealogical information system encoding 
kinship prohibition logic, genetic exogamy, migration history, historical 
demography, onomastics, and socio-political biography — making it an 
unparalleled primary source for South Asian genealogical science.

Gajendra Thakur — 26 
1. Introduction: Genealogy as Science and the Pañji 
Tradition 
1.1 The Genealogical Sciences: A Framework 
Genealogy, in its contemporary interdisciplinary form, encompasses five 
overlapping domains: (i) documentary genealogy — the tracing of descent 
through written records; (ii) genetic genealogy — the use of DNA data 
(autosomal, 
Y-chromosomal, 
mitochondrial) 
to 
establish 
biological 
relationships; (iii) socio-legal genealogy — the use of lineage records for 
property rights, succession, and marriage law; (iv) ethnographic genealogy 
— the study of kinship systems, exogamy rules, and clan identities within 
communities; and (v) computational genealogy — the digitisation, database 
construction, and network analysis of genealogical information. The Pañji-
Prabandha is remarkable because it instantiates all five domains within a 
single, internally consistent tradition. 
From the perspective of modern genealogical theory, the most significant 
challenge any genealogical system faces is what practitioners call the 
'pedigree collapse problem' — the exponential increase in ancestral slots 
across generations that, in practice, collapses into a finite set of actual 
ancestors due to endogamy and limited population size. The Pañji system 
addresses this problem with extraordinary sophistication through its dual 
kinship prohibition mechanism: gotra (patrilineal exogamy) and sapiṇḍa 
(bilateral consanguinity prohibition within defined generational limits). 
1.2 Historical Origin and Institutionalisation 
The Introduction under study, authored by Pañjīkāra Vidyānanda Jha 
'Mohanji' with editorial synthesis by Gajendra Thakur and Nagendra Kumar 
Jha, locates the institutional crystallisation of the Pañji-Prabandha in the 
reign of Mahārāja Harisimhadeva of the Karnata dynasty (r. 1307 AD, b. 
1294 AD). However, it traces the genealogical tradition itself to at least 
Āryabhaṭa (476–550 AD), whose descent line in the Maṇḍara-Kāśyapa mūla 
is documented 39 generations deep. The formal commissioning of the Pañji 
system is precisely dated — using the Śaka-year notation 1248 (= 1326 AD) 
— when Mithilā's paṇḍitas collectively determined the current structural 
form of the register. 
Key Genealogical Timeline: Pañji-Prabandha Institutional History

Vidyānanda's guru-father 2009/2012: Genome Mapping Part 2 — modern 
critical edition published 
1.3 The Document Under Study 
The Bhūmikā (Introduction) to Part 2 of Genome Mapping constitutes an 
exceptional multi-register scholarly document. It combines: (a) institutional 
history of the Pañji from 450 AD to 2009; (b) a technical glossary of Pañji 
terminology (pāribhāṣika śabdāvalī); (c) a sample analytical working (vākya-
vyākhyā) demonstrating how to decode a Pañji entry; (d) a full catalogue of 
all 20 gotras of Maithil Brāhmaṇas with their mūlas (sub-clans), in both 
standard and vernacular pronunciations; (e) detailed mūla-grāma (ancestral 
village) records for major mūlas; (f) ethno-historical surveys of seven 
parganas (Śrīpura, Naveli/Daśirā, Āsaṃgā, Dharampur, etc.); and (g) a 
theoretical disquisition on the dharmashāstric and genetic foundations of 
marriage prohibition. This remarkable breadth makes the document a 
primary source for genealogical theory applied to South Asia. 
2. Structural Architecture of the Pañji System: A 
Genealogical Analysis 
2.1 The Tripartite Information Layer 
Contemporary genealogical database theory distinguishes three information 
layers in any genealogical system: the identity layer (who a person is), the 
relationship layer (how persons are connected), and the attribute layer (what 
is known about them). The Pañji-Prabandha encodes all three layers with 
exceptional density. 
Information Layer | Pañji Element | Genealogical Theory Equivalent

2.2 The Gotra System as Genealogical Framework 
The Pañji recognises 20 gotras among Maithil Brāhmaṇas. In genealogical

to what kinship theorists call a 'phratry' or 'sib' (Rivers 1914; Murdock 1949). 
The document's catalogue of all 20 gotras with their constituent mūlas — 
presented in both Sanskrit (maulik uccāraṇa) and vernacular (laukik 
uccāraṇa) forms — constitutes the most complete taxonomic map of Maithil 
Brāhmaṇa patrilineal organisation available in any single source.

2.3 The Mūla as the Core Genealogical Unit 
The mūla (mūla, literally 'root') is the most operationally significant unit in 
the Pañji system. Genealogically, it is a named patrilineal descent group 
defined by a common founding ancestor (bījī-puruṣa, 'seed-person') whose 
residential origin gives the mūla its name. The document defines the mūla 
with conceptual precision: 
mūlaka artha bhela o prācīnatama grāma jata' kono mūlaka prācīnatama 
puruṣā vā bījīpuruṣa vāsa karaita chalāha| 
This is, in genealogical terms, a definition of the 'Most Recent Common 
Ancestor' (MRCA) locality — the place where the MRCA of the mūla group 
resided. The system then distinguishes between the mūla (the original 
designation) and mūlagrāma (the original and subsequently named branch 
villages), creating what modern genealogists call a 'cluster' or 'pedigree 
family' system. 
Of particular theoretical interest is the document's observation that some 
mūlas 
take 
their 
names 
from 
villages 
in 
Madhya 
Pradesh 
(Khaṇḍavalā/Khaṇḍvā) and Rajasthan — indicating that migration out of 
Mithila and return migration both occurred, and the genealogical system 
preserved these extra-regional origin claims across many centuries. 
2.4 The Ḍūṣaṇa-Pañjī: Genealogical Record of Transgression 
One of the most theoretically significant aspects of the Pañji system is its 
ḍūṣaṇa-pañjī — the defect register. In genealogical terms, this constitutes 
what might be called a 'negative attribute genealogy' — a systematic record 
of lineages that deviated from normative marriage rules, occupation codes, or 
ritual standards. The document explicitly states that the ḍūṣaṇa-pañjī 
recorded marriages across caste lines (including with Muslims, after the 
Islamic conquests), inter-jāti marriages, and widow remarriage with 
documentary evidence. The author notes with emphasis: 
ai dūṣaṇa pañjīme brāhmaṇaka āna jāti khāsa ka' dalita ā muslimaka saṃga 
vivāha, vā pitṛka mṛtyuka bāda vivāhaka likhita dastāveja chala|

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 30 
This is genealogically revolutionary. The Pañji did not simply record 
normative genealogies; it documented deviation, transgression, and socially 
stigmatised relationships — making it a far richer historical source than 
conventional hagiographic genealogies. The ḍūṣaṇa-pañjī thus anticipates, by 
many centuries, the modern genealogical principle that all relationships, 
including those outside normative kinship structures, must be documented 
for historical completeness. 
3. The Pañji and Genetic Genealogy: The Chromosome 
Theory of Marriage Prohibition 
3.1 Sapiṇḍa Prohibition and the Theory of Chromosomal Identity 
The Bhūmikā contains the document's most theoretically ambitious section: a 
sustained argument that the dharmashāstric rules of sapiṇḍa (consanguinity 
prohibition) are genealogically and genetically valid. The document cites the 
Manusmṛti injunction: 
pañcamāt saptamāt udhvaṃ mātṛtaḥ pitṛtastathā| sapiṇḍatā nivarteta sarva 
varṇeṣvayaṃ vidhiḥ|| 
This rule — prohibiting marriage within five generations on the mother's side 
and seven on the father's side — is then explicitly correlated with the modern 
genetic principle of 'Theory of Chromosomes' (the document uses this exact 
English phrase). The argument is as follows: 
The Pañji's Genetic Argument for Sapiṇḍa Prohibition (as stated in the 
Bhūmikā) 
The śāstric sapiṇḍa rules correspond to the genetic principle that in 
chromosomal recombination, harmful recessive alleles shared by closely 
related individuals become expressed in offspring. After five generations 
(maternal) and seven generations (paternal), the coefficient of relationship 
drops below the threshold where such expression is statistically significant. 
The document states: 'saptama (saṃśodhita aṣṭama) o pāñcama pīḍhīk bāda 
samasta guṇasūthatāk nivāraṇa hoita naik' — after the revised seventh and 
fifth generation, all deleterious chromosomal trait concentration is dissipated. 
This is a precise pre-modern statement of what genetics now calls the 
'coefficient of relationship' (r) dropping below critical thresholds at 5th/7th 
degree cousinship.

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 31 
3.2 The Five Marriage Prohibitions and Their Genetic Correlates 
The document enumerates five principal prohibitions (pañca-nideśa) 
governing marriage selection in the Pañji system, each of which can be 
mapped to a modern genetic or genealogical principle: 
Pañji Prohibition (Nideśa) | Modern Genealogical / Genetic Correlate 
1. Same jāti (svajāti): Intra-jāti marriage is the baseline requirement | 
Endogamy boundary — maintains group genetic identity and social cohesion 
2. Gotra prohibition (samagotra): No marriage within the same patrilineal 
clan | Y-chromosomal exogamy — avoids patrilineal inbreeding; equivalent 
to 'sib exogamy' (Murdock 1949) 
3. Pravara prohibition (sama-guru): No marriage if pravara (sacrificial 
lineage) overlaps | Extended patrilineal exogamy — pravara extends the 
prohibition beyond strict patrilineal descent to include associated ritual 
lineages; blocks coefficient of relationship at deeper levels 
4. Kāṣṭha-māmula: Prohibition on marriage with stepmother's brother's 
children | Avoidance of uterine half-sibling relatedness through maternal 
link 
5. Mātṛ-sapiṇḍa: No marriage within 5 maternal generations | Bilateral 
kinship prohibition — equivalent to the genetic threshold for autosomal 
shared DNA dropping below 1.56% (5th cousin relationship, r=0.0156) 
3.3 The Pañji as a Pre-Modern Population Genetics Management 
System 
Viewed from contemporary population genetics, the Pañji system is best 
understood as an institutionalised population genetics management protocol. 
It simultaneously enforced: (a) endogamy at the jāti level — maintaining 
group genetic coherence; (b) exogamy at the gotra and pravara level — 
preventing patrilineal inbreeding; and (c) bilateral consanguinity avoidance 
through sapiṇḍa rules — preventing expression of deleterious recessive 
alleles. The result, over 1,500+ years of continuous practice, would be a 
population with lower inbreeding coefficients than socially comparable 
groups while maintaining cultural cohesion. The document's authors 
explicitly invoke this analogy: 'jīva-vijñāna meṃ guṇa-sūtaka siddhānta 
(Theory of Chromosomes) ehi rūpak achi.' 
This represents a remarkable instance of convergence between indigenous 
systematic knowledge and modern genetic science — not metaphorical

convergence but precise, quantifiable convergence at the level of 
generational threshold mathematics. 
4. The Pañji as Documentary Genealogy: Methodological 
Analysis 
4.1 Documentary Standards and Evidence Hierarchy 
Documentary genealogy, as practiced in Western traditions (NGSQ system, 
GPS — Genealogical Proof Standard), requires: (1) reasonably exhaustive 
search; (2) complete and accurate citations; (3) analysis and correlation of 
evidence; (4) resolution of conflicting evidence; and (5) soundly reasoned, 
coherent written conclusion. The Pañji system, while operating within 
different epistemological conventions, fulfils analogous requirements: 
Genealogical Proof Standard (GPS) | Pañji Equivalent Practice 
1. Reasonably exhaustive search | Pañjīkāra conducted village-to-village 
surveys (kṣetra-bhramaṇa) at Śuddhi seasons; Saurath Sabhā provided 
centralised verification 
2. Complete and accurate citations | Mūla + mūlagrāma + individual name + 
father + śvaśura (father-in-law) + śvaśura's mūla as complete citation unit 
3. Analysis and correlation | Cross-referencing of gotra, pravara, sapiṇḍa, 
kāṣṭha-māmula across Śākhā-pustaka (branch-books) and Utteḍha records 
4. Resolution of conflicting evidence | Ḍūṣaṇa-pañjī mechanism — deviant 
entries were not suppressed but formally recorded as defects 
5. Coherent written conclusion | Siddhānta — the Pañjīkāra's formal 
pronouncement on eligibility at Śuddhi (marriage negotiations), legally 
binding 
4.2 The Pañji Notation System: A Genealogical Language 
The Bhūmikā devotes substantial attention to decoding the Pañji's notation 
system — what can be understood as a compact genealogical encoding 
language. This is significant because it represents a pre-modern solution to a 
perennial challenge in genealogical documentation: how to record complex 
multi-generational relationship data in minimal space while maintaining 
unambiguous readability. The key encoding conventions are: 
Pañji Notation System: Genealogical Encoding Conventions 
Suta/Sutau/Sutā (suta/sutau/sutā): One son / Two sons / Three or more sons 
— a grammatical gender-and-number system for encoding sibship size

Gajendra Thakur — 33 
Prathama / Aparā (ādyā / aparā): First wife's children / Second wife's children 
— encodes polygyny relationships Saṃ (sa~): 'Originally from' — marks the 
mūla (e.g., 'Sonarpur saṃ' = 'of Sonarpur mūla') Nau. (ddau. = ddau.): 
Maternal grandson (nātiduhitā-putra) — records matrilineal descent link for 
sapiṇḍa computation Prākhyāta/Prasiddha (pra. = pra.): 'Known as' 
(sobriquet) — disambiguates individuals with identical names Sudhā 
(sudhāca): 'Also daughters' — acknowledges daughters without naming them 
(patriarchal 
limitation, 
but 
presence 
acknowledged) 
 
M.M.U. 
(Mahāmahopādhyāya): Highest academic title — prosopographical attribute 
encoding Nau. (nau./nepathya): Father-in-law record — cross-referenced to 
establish sapiṇḍa on wife's side 
The document provides a fully worked example of decoding a Pañji entry, 
demonstrating how a single compressed sentence encodes: (a) the 
individual's gotra, mūla, and mūlagrāma; (b) his father's identity; (c) his 
sobriquet; (d) the number, names, and birth order of his children; (e) his 
wives' origins by mūla and mūlagrāma; (f) his father-in-law's identity; and 
(g) his grandchildren's identities through both sons and daughters. This 
density of information in a single Pañji sentence is unmatched in any 
comparable pre-modern genealogical system. 
4.3 The Śākhā-Pustaka: The Branch-Book as Genealogical 
Database 
The Bhūmikā mentions numerous distinct śākhā-pustaka (branch-books) 
maintained by different Pañjīkāra lineages: those of Chotī Jhā (Devānanda 
Jhā, 1760–76), Bhikhiyā Jhā, Jhūlānanda Jhā, Mohanānanda Jhā, Nirasu Jhā 
(compiled at Saurath, 1925–35), and others. In contemporary genealogical 
database terms, each śākhā-pustaka constitutes a 'federated database node' — 
an independently maintained but mutually corroborating record set. The 
Saurath Sabhā functioned as the synchronisation hub where these federated 
nodes were reconciled against each other. 
The document lists the distinct information types recoverable from these 
śākhā-pustaka: (i) gotra-genealogy (gotra-pañjī) — clan and subclan records; 
(ii) aprasiddha-pañjī — records of persons not publicly known, with 
mūlagrāma; (iii) ḍūṣaṇa-pañjī — defect records; and (iv) contemporary 
titulary usage. Together these form what would now be recognised as a 
multi-register genealogical database with cross-referenced indices.

5. Historical Demography and Migration Genealogy 
5.1 The Pañji as Migration Record 
One of the most significant but under-theorised aspects of the Pañji system is 
its function as a migration genealogy — a record of population movement 
across time and space. The document is explicit that mūlagrāma records 
preserve the memory of population dispersals across at least seven centuries: 
Villages recorded as mūlagrāma include settlements in present-day 
Madhubani, Sitamarhi, Vaishali, Darbhanga, Muzaffarpur, Saharsa, Supaul, 
Munger, Purnia, and even regions of Madhya Pradesh (Khaṇḍvā district) and 
Uttarakhand (Garhwal). The document records that during the 13th–18th 
century Islamic invasions and political disruptions, large numbers of Maithil 
Brāhmaṇa families emigrated to Bengal, Rajputana, Braj, Orissa, and 
Garhwal — and the Pañji preserved their points of origin and return. 
Migration Genealogy: Pañji Records of Diaspora and Return 
The Bhūmikā documents several distinct migration waves: Wave 1 (13th–
14th c.): Dispersal during Karnata dynasty's consolidation and Islamic 
incursions; families to Bengal, Uttarakhand, Rajputana, Orissa Wave 2 
(15th–16th c.): Khaṇḍavalā rule's hierarchical impositions caused further 
dispersals; M.M.U. Śaṃkarṣaṇa Ṭhākura founded school in Khaṇḍvā (MP); 
his eighth-generation descendant Ṭhakkura Surapi returned to Mithila Wave 
3 (16th–18th c.): Purnia region settlement — Samara Siṃha of Suragaṇa 
Louāma dynasty received displaced Maithil Brāhmaṇas; 24 mūlas 
documented settling in Purnia during this period Wave 4 (19th c.): Bengal-
resident families returned to Daśirā (Naveli) pargana due to famines; 
Gangolīvāra Nigoni mūla Rāya family documented Preservation mechanism: 
The mūla name preserved origin identity even after generations of residence 
elsewhere; the Pañji system allowed re-integration upon return 
5.2 The Pañji and Historical Demography 
The Bhūmikā's pargana surveys constitute a form of historical demography 
embedded within genealogical narrative. The surveys of Śrīpura Pargana, 
Naveli (Daśirā), Āsaṃgā, Dharampur, and Sauripura reconstruct the social 
history of specific micro-regions through the lens of family genealogies. This 
is precisely what historical demographers call 'family reconstitution' — the 
technique pioneered by Louis Henry and Edward Wrigley in European 
contexts — applied centuries earlier in South Asia through an indigenous 
tradition.

The document notes, for example, the survival or extinction of entire 
villages: 'Sauriyā, Kālasara, Dumara, Bhārīḍīha, Babhangāma ādi gāma lupta 
bha' gela' — Sauriyā (former Suragaṇa Louāma capital), Kālasara, Dumara, 
Bhārīḍīha, and Babhangāma are now extinct settlements. This extinction 
data, recoverable only through the Pañji, constitutes genealogical evidence 
for historical demographic collapse in specific micro-regions during specific 
centuries. 
5.3 Genealogical Evidence for Political History 
The Bhūmikā's genealogical material repeatedly intersects with political 
history in ways that illuminate previously obscure events. The document 
records, for instance, that after the Battle of Kaṃdhīghāṭ (1680–1700 AD), 
Mahārāja Haranārāyaṇa of Dasrā Basaiṭī branch was relocated from 
Farbisganaj to Khaṇḍakhora-Kaṭiyār — a political displacement recorded 
genealogically. The Darbhanga Rāja's formal orders (virudhāvalī) are 
preserved in the Pañji, as are the genealogies of the Suragaṇa Louāma and 
Banaili Rāja families documented with the same precision as Brāhmaṇa 
lineages. 
This demonstrates a feature of the Pañji unique among genealogical systems: 
it is not confined to a single social stratum. While centred on Brāhmaṇas, it 
cross-references Kāyastha and Kṣatriya genealogies, and includes the royal 
genealogies of Mithila as integral to the system — because royal patronage 
and political events shaped the conditions of Brāhmaṇa migration and 
settlement. 
6. Socio-Legal Genealogy: The Pañji as Jurisprudential 
Instrument 
6.1 The Legal Function of the Pañji 
In socio-legal genealogy, records serve as evidence for rights, entitlements, 
and prohibitions. The Pañji is perhaps the world's most elaborate example of 
genealogy as legal instrument. The document is explicit that the Pañji's 
primary social function was juridical: to provide authoritative determination 
of marriage eligibility. The Pañjīkāra's siddhānta (formal determination) at 
the Śuddhi ceremony was legally binding.

The Bhūmikā documents a dramatic legal case that gave rise to the Pañji's 
institutionalisation: the case of Dharmaśāstra scholar Haridatta Upādhyāya, 
who married into the Gaṃgora mūla unknowingly violating sapiṇḍa rules. 
His wife, upon discovering this, took the matter to the royal court of 
Mahārāja Harisimhadeva. She was initially found guilty through the penance 
(prāyaścitta) 
procedures, 
but 
subsequently 
vindicated 
through 
the 
intervention of the jurist Lakhimā Ṭhakurāin — identified as the daughter of 
Mimāṃsaka Ravi Miśra of the Hariharā Mahāoni mūla and wife of M.M. 
Rāmeśvara of Alayavāra mūla. Lakhimā emended the manta text itself to 
correct a doctrinal error. 
This story is genealogically significant because it demonstrates that the Pañji 
system arose not from bureaucratic imposition but from a specific legal crisis 
requiring genealogical information to resolve. The institutionalisation of the 
Pañji was thus a response to a concrete socio-legal failure — the breakdown 
of oral genealogical memory under demographic and political stress. 
6.2 The Pañji as Evidence of Feminist Genealogical Consciousness 
The document explicitly highlights a feature of the Pañji that is remarkable in 
any pre-modern genealogical system: the consistent recording of women's 
names across generations. The author notes: 
puruṣa pradhāna samāja me nārīka nāmollekha vismṛta bhae jāeta achi se 
pañjī prabandha pīr̤ hī sa~ pīr̤ hī dhari nārī nāmaka ullekha kaene jāita achi| 
This is a significant genealogical principle: women are not invisible in the 
Pañji. Daughters are recorded as 'sudhā' (also daughters), their marriage 
destinations are documented, and their sons (the nāti/nau. relationship) are 
tracked as part of the maternal sapiṇḍa computation. This means the Pañji 
preserves bilateral kinship information — both patrilineal and matrilineal — 
making it genealogically far richer than purely agnatic genealogical systems. 
The document's special mention of Lakhimā Ṭhakurāin as 'viduṣī 
dharmaśāstrasya 
vyākhyātri 
alaukika 
pratibhasampanna 
ojasvi 
vaktṛtādhikāriṇī' (a learned woman, commentator on dharmaśāstra, of 
extraordinary talent and eloquent authority) signals a self-conscious 
awareness within the Pañji tradition of women's intellectual and juridical role 
— exceptional for a 14th-century institutional framework.

6.3 The Śraiddhara and Intellectual Property in Genealogy 
The Bhūmikā contains a lengthy complaint about plagiarism — Dr. 
Yogānanda Jhā republishing the 2009 Genome Mapping volume under his 
own name ('Pañjī-Prabandha — Vaṃśa Paricaya, Prathama Bhāga, 2010'), 
and Suśīlā Jhā translating Lakṣmīnātha Jhā's 1962 volume ('Mithilā kī 
Sāṃskṛtik Lokacitrakala') under her own name in 2008. While this is 
presented as an authorial grievance, it raises a significant genealogical theory 
point: intellectual genealogy (the chain of scholarly transmission) is as 
important to the Pañji tradition as biological genealogy. The document 
explicitly names the guru-śiṣya (teacher-student) chain as a form of 
genealogical relationship — Pañjīkāra Vidyānanda Jhā's transmission from 
his father is presented as a form of genealogical inheritance parallel to 
biological descent. 
7. Computational Genealogy and the Pañji's Digital Future 
7.1 The Pañji as Genealogical Database 
The Bhūmikā lists an extensive set of digital resources already created 
around the Pañji: the Ḍūṣaṇa-pañjī scanned PDF (Mithilākṣara single PDF), 
the Mohanānanda Jhā Śākhā-pañjī, the Maṇḍāra-Marāḍe Kāśyapa Prācīna 
(complete), Prācīna Pañjī (laminated), Utteḍha Pañjī, Panicobhe-Bīrpur PDF, 
Darbhanga Rāja Order (Utteḍha), Chotī Jhā Pustaka Nideśikā, Patra Pañjī, 
Mūlagrāma Pañjī (multiple volumes, 1–7), and the box.net hosted Pañjī-Pothi 
archive. This represents a significant digitisation effort that transforms the 
Pañji from a closed oral-documentary tradition into a potentially queryable 
genealogical database. 
From the perspective of computational genealogy, the Pañji's structure is 
exceptionally well-suited to graph database representation. Each individual 
node would carry: personal name, father link, mother link (through wife's 
father), gotra, mūla, mūlagrāma, titles, and sapiṇḍa-relevant ancestor links. 
Marriage edges would carry: wife's name (where recorded), wife's mūla, 
wife's mūlagrāma, wife's father's name. The result would be a genealogical 
graph of potentially millions of nodes spanning 1,500+ years.

7.2 Calibration of Generational Intervals: The Pañji's Internal 
Chronology 
The document demonstrates an elegant internal chronological calibration 
method: since Āryabhaṭa's birth-year (476 AD) is known, and the Pañji 
records him as 39 generations before a known reference point in the 
Maṇḍara-Kāśyapa mūla, the average generation interval can be calculated. 
Similarly, Gohū Jhā (c. 1050–1150 AD) is recorded as 24th generation from 
Āryabhaṭa in the Vatsa lineage, and Vidyāpati (c. 1350 AD) as 14th 
generation from the same anchor in the Visfī-Kāśyapa mūla. 
Using the formula: generation interval = (known date₂ − known date₁) / 
(generational depth₂ − generational depth₁), the internal generation interval in 
Pañji calculations is approximately 40 years. The document explicitly 
confirms this: 'ek pīḍhīkeṃ 40 saṃ guṇā kelā saṃ Āryabhaṭṭak janma 
lagabhaga 476 ī. ā Vidyāpatik janma lagabhaga 1350 ī. abaita achi je 
itihāsasammata achi.' This self-validation of the Pañji's chronological 
methodology against externally known dates is a sophisticated genealogical 
proof technique. 
7.3 The Pañji and FamilySearch / Genealogical Database 
Standards 
The Pañji's encoding conventions can be mapped to contemporary 
genealogical data standards (GEDCOM 5..1/7., FHISO BDM) with relatively 
straightforward transformations. The suta/sutau/sutā encoding maps to 
CHIL/HUSB/WIFE relationships; the mūla/mūlagrāma maps to place-of-
origin fields; the sapiṇḍa constraint matrix maps to relationship prohibition 
rules that modern software (e.g., RootsMagic, Gramps) could theoretically 
encode. The Pañji's notation for 'prathama' (first wife) and 'aparā' (second 
wife) maps directly to GEDCOM's FAM record sequence. A full GEDCOM 
conversion of even a single major śākhā-pustaka would create a genealogical 
resource of extraordinary scholarly value.

8. Comparative Genealogical Analysis: The Pañji in World 
Context 
8.1 Comparative Framework 
To properly assess the Pañji's significance, it must be placed in comparative 
context with other major world genealogical traditions. The following 
analysis compares the Pañji across seven dimensions with the four most 
significant non-Indian genealogical traditions: 
Dimension | Pañji (Mithila) | Mormon (FamilySearch) | Imperial China 
(Zúpǔ) | Islamic Nasab | Icelandic Sagas 
Continuity (years) | 1,500+ documented | ~175 (since 1844) | ~2,000 
(fragmented) | ~1,400 (selective) | ~800 (literary) 
Coverage (persons) | Millions (Maithil Brāhmaṇas) | Billions (all humans) 
| Hundreds of millions (Han Chinese) | Selective elites & scholars | 
~50,000 (Iceland) 
Exogamy enforcement | Active (gotra + sapiṇḍa) | Passive (incest laws 
only) | Clan exogamy (surname) | No formal system | Minimal 
Women recorded | Yes (bilateral) | Yes (all) | Minimal (agnatic bias) | 
Minimal (agnatic bias) | Partial (maternal lines) 
Defect/transgression records | Yes (Ḍūṣaṇa-pañjī) | Partial (DNA 
contradictions) | Rarely (stigmatised) | Rarely | Yes (saga narrative) 
Legal/juridical function | Primary function | Secondary (LDS theology) | 
Property succession | Inheritance, Ashraf status | Minimal post-medieval 
Genetic validation | Intrinsic (sapiṇḍa math) | DNA testing separate | DNA 
testing separate | None | None 
Digitisation status | Partial (Videha project) | Comprehensive | Partial 
(Jiapu databases) | Minimal | Complete (Íslendingabók) 
8.2 The Pañji's Unique Theoretical Contributions 
The comparative analysis reveals several features in which the Pañji is 
genuinely unique among world genealogical traditions: 
First, the Pañji is the only genealogical system that operates as a real-time 
marriage clearance mechanism rather than a retrospective record. The 
Pañjīkāra's siddhānta at the Śuddhi must be obtained before marriage 
negotiations can proceed — making the genealogist a legally necessary actor 
in social reproduction, not merely a historical archivist.
